data.name: Kim Seung-gyu
data.description: Decided to become a footballer aged eight after being captivated by the legendary South Korean keeper Kim Byung-ji, who famously scored a header during a K League playoff. His dad discouraged him from becoming a keeper at first, and his early years were all spent outfield, but once he switched he never looked back. He spent three years in the Saudi Pro League with Al-Shabab before moving to Tokyo last year. He named Lee Woon-jae – hero of the 2002 World Cup and Kim Byung-ji’s greatest rival – as his role model, and, just as the Kim-Lee rivalry defined an era for the national side, he has spent the last decade as a fierce rival for the national No 1 spot with his friend Jo Hyeon-woo. In May 2024, Kim married the South Korean supermodel Kim Jin-kyung.

data.name: Lee Han-beom
data.description: An FC Seoul graduate, Lee’s career took off after 2023’s move to Denmark with Midtjylland, where his progress earned him a spot in the South Korea senior setup. While his international chances have been sporadic so far, his imposing physical profile and fine reading of the game have had plenty eyeing him as a potential successor to Kim Min-jae. He won gold at the 2022 Hangzhou Asian Games, which provided an exemption from mandatory military service. He spoke last year about where he needs to develop, telling Fifa.com: “What I lack is aggression in my play. When we’re in national team camp, Kim Min-jae often tells me: ‘Centre-backs need to be rough.’ I’ve been trying to meet that expectation, but I think it’s still a work in progress, although I believe that I’ve improved in that area quite a bit.”

data.name: Lee Gi-hyuk
data.description: In a final squad selection that offered few surprises, the defender’s inclusion stands out as the sole genuine wildcard. Comfortable in possession, the left-footed Lee is handy in building from the back and has a natural willingness to drive forward, meaning he is also expected to provide cover as a defensive midfielder. Lee first entered the professional ranks as a midfielder but was, by most accounts, a peripheral figure. However, after he moved to Gangwon, their manager, Yoon Jong-hwan, converted him into a defender – a decision that propelled his career forward, transforming him into one of the K League’s finest centre-backs. Lee was called up by South Korea and had to do some homework. "I want to watch Hwang In-beom closely and learn as much as I can from him," he said. Interestingly, his childhood footballing blueprint was Manchester United’s utility man John O’Shea. Off the pitch, Lee boasts a diverse athletic background as a taekwondo black belt.

data.name: Kim Min-jae
data.description: Widely regarded as Asia’s finest centre-back, “the Monster” gets his physicality from his judo-trained dad and his pace from his mum, a track and field athlete. Kim’s club career has been stellar: in 2023 he was named Serie A’s best defender when playing for Napoli, whose then coach, Luciano Spalletti, said: “He does at least 20 unbelievable things per game. When he runs with the ball at his feet, he's inside the opposition penalty area within five seconds." Soon after that award he made a £43m move to Bayern Munich, juggling that with completing his compulsory military service. The move made him the most expensive Asian footballer of all time. After the 2022 World Cup, Son Heung-min announced during a dinner that Kim was the side’s best-looking player. Kim responded: “I don’t trust people who say I’m handsome.”

data.name: Kim Tae-hyeon
data.description: A left-footed centre-back whose impressive form in the J-League earned him a first call-up last summer, and ultimately a place in this World Cup squad. He would have moved to Japan much sooner after a deal was agreed with Vegalta Sendai in December 2021, but the transfer stalled because of Covid. It was revived the next year, though, and after a successful spell there he joined Sagan Tosu in 2024, then moved to his current club, Kashima Antlers, last year. He’s affectionately known as “Swings” in the squad, thanks to his resemblance to the South Korean rapper.

data.name: Hwang In-beom
data.description: Hwang became a cornerstone of the national team under Paulo Bento, who handed him a starting role after his impressive performance in the 2018 Asian Games gold medal run, and he will be key to any success the side have this summer. A hugely well-travelled player, his career path began in Daejeon before moves to Vancouver Whitecaps, Rubin Kazan, FC Seoul, Olympiakos, Red Star Belgrade and, in 2024, to Feyenoord. A versatile box-to-box midfielder, he suffered an injury scare in March, damaging ankle ligaments in a game against Excelsior, but recovered in time to make the cut. His favourite career game so far? “Korea's game against Portugal in the final World Cup group stage match in 2022, and that will never change. That was the most dramatic game that I've ever played in, and the result [2-1 for Korea] made me the happiest person. I pride myself in being a footballer who helped to create a result that made all of our people in Korea happy.”

data.name: Son Heung-min
data.description: 2025 was a big year for Son. As the captain who finally broke Tottenham’s trophy curse, he ended a decade in north London with a £20m move to Los Angeles – an MLS record – amid a blizzard of “east versus west” marketing pitting Lionel Messi in the Eastern Conference versus Son in the Western. He remains a superb talent, though there was some criticism in the buildup to the World Cup that he has started losing his edge. Son hit back, saying: “I don't think I've suffered any dip in my performance. It's disappointing that people talk about decline whenever I hit a dry spell. I know there are high expectations on me because I've scored so many goals over the years. I am always doing the best I can, and I am feeling good physically." In 2022 he launched his own fashion brand, NOS7, standing for “Nothing, Ordinary Sunday”, with the brand ethos focused on reflection and calm at the weekend: “Weekend after a busy week, we share that precious moment.”

data.name: Paik Seung-ho
data.description: A gifted player who made waves aged 13, winning the Cha Bum-kun award for the top young prospect in the country, and joining Barcelona’s La Masia academy. The weight of expectation was huge, but his progress was hit when Fifa imposed a transfer ban on Barcelona for breaching transfer and registration rules around signing international players aged under 18, barring him from competitive action during his formative years. He left the club in 2017 for Girona, then moved to Germany with Darmstadt in 2019. He returned to Korea with Jeonbuk Hyundai Motors in 2021 before a move to Birmingham in 2024. Last year he discussed the impact English football had made on him, saying in an interview with the University of Birmingham: “Moving to England, I realise football means so much. It's never just a game – it's everything to this city, for everyone here. It can bring you down hard, but it gives you a rush like nothing else. That's what makes football such a massive sport, and I've truly come to feel that way – it's why I keep pushing to play with a full heart." He made his international debut in 2019, and scored a brilliant goal against Brazil during the 2022 World Cup.

data.name: Cho Gue-sung
data.description: Cho’s journey to becoming an international striker began late, during his first year at Gwangju University, when he moved up front from his normal defensive midfield position. He caught the eye in 2019 as he scored 14 goals for FC Anyang in K League 2, but his transition to the top-flight with Jeonbuk in 2020 was a struggle. It was during his military service that he rediscovered his scoring touch, proving prolific during 2021-22 with the K League 1 military side Gimcheon Sangmu, and performing at international level too, scoring twice against Ghana at the Qatar World Cup, which he later called “the best moment of my life”. He joined the Danish side Midtjylland in 2023, hitting double figures in his first season, but then missing the whole of the following season after complications with his knee surgery. He made his comeback in August last year after a gruelling 448-day hiatus. “When I first realised how serious my injury was, the situation was obviously difficult, but I quickly decided to just start afresh,” he said last year. “That was my mentality, because my lifestyle is not about regretting things from the past … I’m not an emotional person.”

data.name: Lee Jae-sung
data.description: A genuine South Korea legend with more than 100 caps. He traded his K League MVP status for Germany in 2018, joining Holstein Kiel before moving to Mainz in 2021. Despite his slender frame he is a formidable aerial specialist, a prolific scorer with his head. He knows time is catching up with him, and said in March: “It's the last and my third World Cup, but there's nothing particularly different about this one. I've always felt the same. The World Cup was a stage I dreamed of since childhood. I think each day will be precious. I'll do my best in the remaining time. If I play, I hope it will be an enjoyable time. Of course we have to achieve results, but I want to spend enjoyable time with the players rather than suffering under pressure. I want to have a good time in front of the many citizens who support us.” He’s known affectionately as Strawberry Milk thanks to a spirited dance performance with the girl group at a K League awards ceremony.

data.name: Hwang Hee-chan
data.description: Like Son Heung-min, Hwang began his professional career in Europe, which is unusual for South Korean players. He moved from Red Bull Salzburg to RB Leipzig in 2020, then to Wolves in 2022 after a successful loan spell. His nickname, the Bull, is a nod to both his first club and his aggressive playing style. He rose to international prominence during his time at Salzburg, famously scoring against Liverpool at Anfield in the Champions League. He maintains a close friendship with his former teammate Erling Haaland. In a 2020 South Korea friendly against Qatar he scored just 16 seconds after kickoff, setting the record for the fastest goal in South Korean international history. In 2023, Pep Guardiola was criticised for referring to him as “the Korean guy” rather than by name before a Manchester City match against Wolves; Hwang responded by scoring the winner the following day. He said later: “I heard ‘the Korean guy’, and some people had negative views. But I always think positively. I used it to motivate myself a lot.”

data.name: Song Bum-keun
data.description: Song became the starting keeper for the then K League 1 champions, Jeonbuk Hyundai Motors, at 21, and has since lifted the title five times. But, due to his international teammate Jo Hyeon-woo playing in the same division, it wasn't until last year that he finally earned a spot in the K League 1 Best XI as the league's top keeper. Well respected for his excellent distribution and composure in one-on-one situations, he has a big future with the national side. His name was inspired by the South Korean football legend Cha Bum-kun, a two-time Uefa Cup winner, as his father was a big fan. This also led to his nickname, Song-boom, a play on Cha's nickname, Cha-boom.

data.name: Lee Tae-seok
data.description: Lee is often described as the mirror image of his father, Lee Eul-yong, part of South Korea’s 2002 World Cup side. It’s not just the striking physical resemblance: he also has his father’s cultured left foot. So is he better than his dad? “Not yet, but I’m getting there,” he said last year. The elder Lee was a late bloomer but his son’s progress has been fast: he secured both his international debut and a move abroad at a significantly younger age. Under Hong Myung-bo he has cemented his place as the national team’s first-choice left-back, where he wears the No 13 shirt, the same worn by his father. Lee said in 2025: “Things I only dreamed of last year have come true this year – transferring to Europe and playing regularly for the national team. My next goal is to represent Korea at the World Cup. I’ll keep pushing.”

data.name: Cho Wi-je
data.description: A late, late inclusion in the squad after Cho Yu-min left the training camp in tears having torn the plantar fascia in his right heel just days before the tournament. As he left, Cho Yu-min said: “I wanted to help the team and couldn't, so I'm sorry. I'll carry all the bad luck that the team may face back to Korea with me and leave behind only the passion that I prepared with.” Six months ago, the 2001-born Cho Wi-je was operating in the relative obscurity of the domestic second tier, anchoring a mid-table defence for Busan IPark. Yet, the centre-back's raw, multifaceted profile caught the eye of the nation’s heavyweight elite. A winter transfer to Jeonbuk Hyundai Motors followed, bringing with it a whirlwind call-up to the national team’s pre-tournament training camp. Cho, who chose to major in logistics and statistical information during his university days, told reporters in the United States: "This situation feels surreal and unbelievable. It’s hard to feel happy when my heart is heavy for Yu-min. I know no words can console someone in this situation. The rest of us performing well will be the best way to comfort him."

data.name: Kim Moon-hwan
data.description: Spent his formative years as a forward, and stayed there until he moved into professional football. But when he joined Busan IPark in 2017 his potential as an attacking full-back became clear, and since then he has grown into a mainstay of the national side, widely regarded as one of the pre-eminent right-backs in the country. He had a season in the US with Los Angeles FC in 2021-22, and has previously cited the Russian legend Yuri Zhirkov as his inspiration. During his time at Chung-Ang University he forged a formidable, league-winning attacking partnership with Cho Yu-min, now his national team colleague who himself underwent a metamorphosis into a centre-back, but is missing the finals because of injury.

data.name: Park Jin-seob
data.description: A real late bloomer. Until he was 23 Park played in the semi-professional K3 League, but his rise since becoming a professional has been rapid: winning the K League 1 title and earning a deserved call-up to the national side. He brings useful versatility to this summer’s squad, able to operate as a centre-back or a defensive midfielder. Shares his English initials, Park J-s, with a South Korean legend, and is also known for his striking resemblance to his international teammate Joo Min-kyu. But he’s very much an outstanding individual in his own right. Gus Poyet, his former manager at Jeonbuk Hyundai Motors, said after last year’s title win: "He possesses a level of leadership that is rare to find anywhere in the world. To me, Park Jin-seob is the MVP."

data.name: Bae Jun-ho
data.description: A pacey winger who blends the industry of Park Ji-sung with the technical guile of Lee Chung-yong. He has been seen as the natural heir to Son Heung-min and Hwang Hee-chan on the left flank since he first made headlines in South Korea’s semi-final run at the 2023 Under-20 World Cup. Despite scoring on his senior international debut in 2024 he has been in and out of the squad since, but his displays in England with Stoke kept him in contention for this summer. After winning player of the year in his first season at Stoke in 2023-24 he talked about the process of settling in, saying: “Before I learned the language it was very difficult. It was easier when I was able to communicate to a certain extent. The first thing I learned was swearing so I was able to freely say what I thought.” In March this year his club manager, Mark Robins, said all Bae needed to add to his game was more goals “to take him to the top level – and he’s capable of doing that. He’s got loads and loads of good attributes … it’s there, all there, we’ve just got to put it all together more often.”

data.name: Oh Hyeon-gyu
data.description: A product of Suwon Samsung Bluewings' youth academy, Oh’s resilience was tested early on when a cruciate ligament injury sidelined him for nearly an entire year during his first year of high school. It was a long way back, but he made his professional debut with Suwon in 2019, then made himself a club hero in 2022 when he scored the dramatic extra-time winner in the promotion-relegation playoffs, a moment that left the young striker in floods of tears. Having completed his mandatory military service he left for Europe, first to Celtic in 2023, then to Genk a year later. Last summer a proposed £28m move to Stuttgart fell through when he failed the medical, but in February he completed a £12m move to Turkey. In March he reflected on his time at Celtic, his first experience of Europe, and his struggle with homesickness: “When I was at Celtic there was a song I used to listen to quite often, especially when things got a bit tough. That was the song Take Me Home, Country Roads. I listened to it a lot. It gave me some comfort, and it often made me think about how much I wanted to go home. But I haven’t really felt like listening to it lately.”

data.name: Lee Kang-in
data.description: Widely acknowledged as one of the finest left-footed attackers in South Korean history. Lee first became famous aged just six when he starred in the reality football TV show Fly Shoot Dori, which paired kids with former professionals as they learned the game. “I won the show,” he said last year. “I made a lot of friends there, and I have fond memories of it … The coaches instilled in me the idea of not losing easy balls and trying to play forward. I've loved football since I was a child, and so has my family.” He moved to Spain aged 10 to join the Valencia youth academy and made rapid progress through the ranks. He made his senior club debut at 17, then won the Golden Ball at the 2019 Under-20 World Cup. That same year he won Asian young footballer of the year, and made his senior international debut. He joined Paris Saint-Germain for £18m in 2023 where he became the first Asian player to win a continental treble.

data.name: Yang Hyun-jun
data.description: Yang catapulted himself into the spotlight during the summer of 2022. Turning out for the K League All-Stars against Tottenham during the Premier League club’s pre-season tour of South Korea, the tricky winger left Eric Dier chasing shadows before unleashing a ferocious strike – a breakthrough moment to put his name on the map. Yang’s eventual call-up to the senior national team was met with a playful welcome from PSG’s Lee Kang-in. Having held the mantle of the squad's youngest player until Yang's arrival, Lee quipped that he was delighted to finally have "someone new to tease". A high-profile move to Europe beckoned in the summer of 2023, with Yang sealing a transfer to Celtic. The move saw him link up with his compatriot Oh Hyeon-gyu, and the duo are now primed to showcase their synergy on the grandest stage of all at this World Cup. While naturally an out-and-out winger, Yang has recently demonstrated his tactical versatility, deployed as a dynamic wingback for club and country.

data.name: Jo Hyeon-woo
data.description: Jo rose to stardom with a heroic clean sheet against Germany in the 2018 World Cup, a match known as the Miracle of Kazan in Korea. He played it down later, saying: “It wasn't me, but the whole nation that stood together to block them. We defended as one nation." In the years since he has become a key pillar of the national team alongside his rival for the No 1 spot, Kim Seung-gyu. Renowned for his elite reflexes and aerial stability, he has two nicknames: “Dae-Gea” – a mix of his former club Daegu and the player he’s often compared to, David De Gea – and Bit-Hyeon-woo, meaning “Shining Hyeon-woo”, casting him as the country’s saviour. Speaking last year he said the World Cup was “an opportunity to let more people know about a goalkeeper named Jo Hyeon-woo … I'm a little nervous … I think I'll leave Korea with a fluttering heart.”

data.name: Seol Young-woo
data.description: A true local icon, whose schoolboy career, university tenure and professional debut all took place in his home town, Ulsan. A versatile full-back capable of operating on either flank, he has also put in solid performances at centre-back, winger and central midfielder when called upon. His development was being hindered by recurrent shoulder dislocations but successful surgery in 2024 appears to have fixed the problem. He finally left Ulsan in 2024 to join Red Star Belgrade, and has since been linked with moves to England. Throughout his professional career he has exclusively worn the number 66 – a tribute to his father’s birth year and to his admiration for Trent Alexander-Arnold.

data.name: Jens Castrop
data.description: The second German-born player to represent the country, after Cha Du-ri. Born to a German father and a Korean mother, he played for Germany at various youth levels before choosing to switch in August last year, and received his first South Korea call-up a month later. “Growing up I always knew I was not just a regular German like others in my class,” he told fifa.com in March, saying he hoped his decision would contribute to “breaking barriers for more diversity in Korea”: “I always knew that I'm at least half Korean. That's something in your character that you see every day as you handle certain situations. You're just a little bit kind of different than a 100% German child. I always knew I was Korean and I want to help the team and show others that it's possible to change the nation and change the country.” An adaptable performer, he has played mostly in midfield for his country and at full-back for Borussia Mönchengladbach, so brings valuable versatility to this summer’s squad.

data.name: Kim Jin-gyu
data.description: Kim’s international career could not have started much better: he scored in his first two appearances in 2022. But it was a false dawn: he fell out of favour and it took a staggering 34 months for him to return to the senior setup after his last involvement in July 2022. It was only under the guidance of Gus Poyet at Jeonbuk last year that his all-round game improved enough to force a recall. He should be an asset to the squad this summer, with solid forward passing and a threat from set pieces. He told the Korea Times in March: “When it comes to making aggressive passes and managing plays, I've always felt confident that I can be as good as anybody. Since I started getting called up more frequently last year, I began dreaming about making the World Cup team.”

data.name: Eom Ji-sung
data.description: A pacey winger able to operate on both flanks, Eom is a product of the same high school that nurtured the former Swansea star Ki Sung-yueng. When Eom moved to Wales in 2024 from the K League 1 club Gwangju FC he said: “Ki contacted me and we spoke on the phone. It was a big help because Ki cheered me on, told me to work hard, and told me that I would adapt well. I was young at the time when he played for Swansea, so I don't remember much, but I do remember some fantastic mid-range shots and goals. Since I was young, I dreamed of emulating players like Ki Sung-yueng and Park Ji-sung, and I think that was the driving force that brought me to where I am now. I think that’s why I was able to seize such a great opportunity.” Known for using Son Heung-min’s famous camera celebration, he owes his name to having parents who were devoted fans of Park Ji-sung.

data.name: Lee Dong-gyeong
data.description: He and Lee Kang-in are the two most clinical left-footed players in the national side. He joined Ulsan Hyundai after dropping out of university in 2018, and has since had loan spells in Germany with Schalke and Hansa Rostock. Last year, while completing his military service, he was named K League 1 player of the year for his performances with Gimcheon Sangmu, the division’s military club. His nickname, Tokyo Lee, plays on his given name, Dong-gyeong, which shares the same pronunciation as the Korean word for Tokyo. Lee, who was born exactly one year after teammate Hwang In-beom, spoke in January about his one big career ambition: “It is to play in my first World Cup. I’ve aligned everything, my mindset and my condition, towards the World Cup … There’s a lot more I haven’t shown yet. I want to throw the doors wide open to the prime of my football career.”
